title: Chapter 28: The Council Watches
content: The Upper Chamber met in silence.
Seven seats. Seven Primordials. Each one a fragment of the original darkness that existed before the Nether-Grid, before the Exchange, before humanity itself.
Roxanne's seat — the Second Seat — was empty.
"She did not come," said the Fifth Seat. Its voice was the sound of stone grinding against stone. "She was summoned, and she did not come."
"She is busy," said the Seventh Seat. Younger. Softer. Its form was a shifting constellation of faint lights. "She has a territory to manage."
"She has a currency to mint," the First Seat corrected.
The First Seat had no form. It was simply a presence — a weight in the air, a pressure behind the eyes of everyone in the chamber. It had not spoken in centuries. Until today.
"The mortal woman is printing her own currency."
"Backed by the original card," the Fourth Seat added. Its voice was wet. Gurgling. Like something speaking through water. "The Frost-bringer confirmed its validity."
"The Frost-bringer works for her now."
Silence.

"She is demonstrating that our system is optional," the First Seat continued. "If she succeeds, every territory will want its own currency. Every Collector will question their authority. The Nether-Grid will fragment."
"Except her card is the original contract," the Seventh Seat pointed out quietly.
"A relic of a dead era. The Void collapsed. The contract should have collapsed with it."
"You want to remove her."
"I want to contain her. There is a difference."
"What kind of containment?" the Fifth Seat rumbled.
"Economic isolation. The Exchange has agreed to restrict all trade routes into her territory. No reagents. No Nether-Grid materials. No entities willing to contract with her people."
"She has a Frost-bringer. She can manufacture her own reagents."
"The Frost-bringer is one entity. She needs infrastructure. She needs suppliers. She needs —"
"She has the original card. She can buy whatever she wants."
The First Seat's presence flickered. A rare sign of irritation.
"The card is not a factory. It is a payment method. If there is nothing to buy because no one will sell to her, the card becomes irrelevant."
"That assumes she cannot create her own supply chain."
"She is a mortal art student who has been in the Nether-Grid for less than two months. She cannot —"
"She already has."

The Seventh Seat's constellation brightened. "She has a functioning territory with food, water, shelter, and a medical clinic. She has a currency that seven thousand people trust. She has a Frost-bringer handling her accounts. She has the Bound God at her side. And she did all of this without the Council's approval, without the Exchange's support, and without a single Obol of debt."
A pause.
"Perhaps the reason she is dangerous is not that she is weak. Perhaps it is that she has demonstrated how unnecessary we are."
The chamber went cold.
The Third Seat's blade-voice cut through the silence. "You speak as though you admire her."
"I observe her. Admiration is irrelevant."
"Is it?"

The Seventh Seat's lights dimmed slightly. "I made a promise to the Bound God, long ago. Before he was sealed. I told him that if he ever found someone worthy of his loyalty, I would not stand in his way."
"You knew what he was."
"I knew what he could become."
The First Seat's presence pressed down. "The Seventh Seat is reminded that this Council operates on consensus, not sentiment."
"I am aware."
"Then vote."
"On what?"
"On whether to authorize the Exchange's isolation strategy."
The Seventh Seat was silent for a long moment.
Then: "I abstain."
"Abstention is noted. Fourth Seat?"
"In favor."
"Fifth Seat?"
"In favor."
"Third Seat?"
"In favor."
"Sixth Seat?"
A pause. The Sixth Seat had not spoken once this entire session. Its form was a spiral of smoke, slowly turning.
"...Abstain."
"Two abstentions. The motion carries. The isolation strategy is authorized."
The First Seat's presence began to recede.
But before it faded completely, it added one more thing:
"And someone should inform the Second Seat that she missed the vote. Politely."
Roxanne found out about the isolation strategy the same evening.
Frost appeared in her office mid-dinner, its crystalline hand tracing a red line through the air.
"The Council has voted. Economic isolation of the Vance Demesne has been authorized."
Roxanne set down her fork.
"When?"
"This afternoon. You were not present."
"I was teaching an art history class."
"I am aware. I watched. Your lecture on chiaroscuro was well-structured, though your analysis of Caravaggio's use of shadow could have been deeper."
"I'll take notes for next time. What does isolation mean in practice?"
"No trade routes through Exchange-controlled space. No Nether-Grid reagents. No entity contracts. All external suppliers have been instructed to refuse your Infinity Notes."
"So they're trying to starve my economy."
"Correct."
Roxanne picked up her fork again. Took another bite.
"Let them try."
"You are not concerned?"
"I'm concerned. But I'm not panicking. Panicking doesn't solve logistics."
Frost's hood tilted. "You are the most unusual human I have ever encountered."
"I'll take that as a compliment."
"It was intended as one."
Later, Silas found her in the dean's office, surrounded by maps of the Nether-Grid.
"The isolation order came from the First Seat," he said.
"I figured. The First Seat doesn't like me."
"The First Seat doesn't like anything that challenges the established order. You are a challenge incarnate."
She traced a route on the map with her finger. "There are paths through the Nether-Grid that aren't controlled by the Exchange."
"The Deep Paths. They are dangerous."
"Everything is dangerous. That's not a disqualifier."
"They are dangerous even by Nether-Grid standards. Entities live in the Deep Paths that do not recognize the Council's authority or the Exchange's contracts."
"So they're independent."
"They are unregulated. There is a difference."
"To you, maybe. To me, unregulated means untaxed, unmonitored, and available."
Silas was quiet for a moment.
"You want to open trade routes through the Deep Paths."
"I want to open trade routes, period. If the Exchange won't let me use their roads, I'll build my own."
"Through territory where even Primordials fear to tread."
"Then I'll bring a Primordial with me."
She looked up at him.
"You're at 60 percent. How much stronger would you be if I bought you a new set of pathways through the Deep?"
Silas's red eyes glowed faintly.
"Significantly."
"Good. Pack your bags. We're going shopping in the abyss."
